dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 2 responding for breathing problems.
Hello, my name is Jay, and I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What was the issue that got you to call 911 today?
I've been having a lot of trouble breathing for about an hour now with wheezing.
Can you look at me and tell me your name for me?
Yes.
Good. What is your name, please?
It's Daniel.
All right, Daniel. What county are we in today? Can you tell me?
We are in Richmond County.
Nice. You're alert and responding to questions, with eye opening, verbal response, and motor response.
Your airway appears open and patent. Can you continue to breathe without difficulty right now?
Breathing is tough. I have to push for every breath.
Okay, I understand. Your breathing seems fast, I notice some labored movement in your chest and neck. Would you mind if I listen to your lungs?
Mr. Daniel, your respiratory rate is 26. Elevated and quite labored.
Heart rate is 83 right now with a regular rhythm.
Blood pressure is 179 over 110-this is still within normal range.
Oxygen saturation is 94% unless we give you oxygen, which should help increase it further.
Let me check out your skin tone and pulse. Your skin looks warm and moist with normal color.
Radial pulses are present and strong.
You seem very anxious. Let me make sure you're oriented to person, place, time, and situation.
What is your name again?
Daniel... Daniel Smith.
Are you aware of where we are now?
We are in Richmond County, in my home.
Great. You're oriented in all four areas-person, place, time, and situation.
And you're fully awake, alert, and responsive-AOx4.
Your heart rate is increasing now-it's trending toward 120. That's a sign of tachycardia. We're keeping an eye on it.
Can you tell me more about your medical history and what led up to this?
I've had COPD and asthma diagnosed for years.
When did the trouble with breathing start?
About an hour ago it escalated. I had used two nebulizers, but it didn't help.
What kind of weather have we had lately?
It's been raining and very humid.
I see. How severe would you rate your breathing difficulty on a scale from 0 to 10?
An 8.
Does the tightness or pressure travel anywhere else in your body?
It hurts on my left chest-probably from coughing too hard.
Have you had a productive cough or a non-productive one?
A dry, non-productive cough all day.
Your skin looks normal in color, and it's reactive with good turgor.
Let's take a look at your eyes and head for signs.
You appear alert and oriented in all four parts (AOx4).
Pupils are equal and reactive to light.
I need to listen to your lungs and heart. Please stay as still as possible.
Lungs have severe wheezing in both inspiratory and expiratory phases with notable retractions at your neck.
Heart sounds are regular, and there's no swelling or cyanosis noted.
You've mentioned diabetes. Let me check in-have you noticed anything unusual with urination or stools?
No, everything's been normal otherwise.
Are your legs and arms moving smoothly and without pain?
Range of motion seems intact in all limbs-the patient can move all his joints without issue.
The patient has a steady gait and is able to walk without assistance.
We are going to start a nebulizer treatment for you. Do you mind?
Not at all.
The nebulizer includes albuterol and ipratropium. We are also starting you on 8 liters of oxygen.
I'm placing the EKG leads on you. Can I place them directly over your skin?
Sure.
We have you on continuous monitoring. This is standard protocol for respiratory distress.
Daniel, we're treating you as an asthma/COPD exacerbation under our respiratory distress protocol.
We're also starting an IV just in case. We have some powerful medications to give.
Your heart rate is now 95 and your respiratory rate is 19. Vital signs have slightly stabilized.
Blood pressure is 160 over 96, still elevated, but oxygen saturation improved to 99. Glucose was not drawn due to later supplies.
Does the breathing feel better at all now?
A little, but I still need help.
What would you say your breathing difficulty level is now?
A 7, barely.
We are also giving you a 10mg dose of dexamethasone. This can reduce inflammation in your airway.
We've also started you on 2 grams of magnesium sulfate intravenously. This helps relax airway muscles.
After we give you some more quick breathing treatment, we'll check it again.
Daniel, where would you like to go for medical care?
Please take me to VCU Health Systems.
I'm going to secure you safely into the stretcher.
Daniel, I need to give you another albuterol nebulizer treatment based on the current plan.
Do you feel like trying CPAP now as it can help you breathe easier?
No, please don't use the CPAP. It makes it worse.
Your breathing pain level is now a 6. We are continuing care as per our respiratory distress protocol.
Another EKG reading. Still on sinus rhythm but with some nonspecific T-wave changes.
All right, we're rolling and speaking as we move so we stay prepared.
I feel tired now and not as much relief as earlier.
Just know we're here with you through the whole journey. You're in good hands.
Are you okay to proceed with the ride to the hospital?
I trust you. Go ahead.
Vital signs now are stable with some fluctuation. Heart rate is tapping toward 120.
Daniel, can you stay awake for us?
Yes, but it's getting hard to think straight now.
We'll reposition you for your utmost comfort and continue your IV drip and monitoring.
Another dose of albuterol delivered now. Let's give it some time to take effect.
Daniel, how are you doing with the medications? They're all legitimate for your condition.
I feel some relief but still very tired.
We're getting ready for arrival. Heart rate's under control. Sats are good at 99%. Glucose was initially 91 earlier in the call.
We're almost at VCU Health Systems. Can you stay with us until we arrive?
I'll try.
We can do one more albuterol, but not sure it'll change anything. Let's hold it off for now.

I need to mention your complete medical history to the hospital when you arrive. Let them know you have asthma and COPD, as well as diabetes non-insulin dependent, primary hypertension, major depression, anxiety, and schizoaffective disorder in your history.
Also, we need to note your peanut allergy for the medical team to be aware.
VCU Health Systems is the one I want to go to.
We really hope your breathing gets full and comfortable at the hospital. Let's keep doing our part.
Hospital, this is EMT Unit 2 with a 60-year-old male with COPD and asthma exacerbation. We are approximately 5 minutes out with vitals stable and set for handoff.
We've done four nebulizers, administered dexamethasone, magnesium sulfate, and placed a 12-lead EKG.
Arrived now, preparing for transition.
We are transitioning to transfer care having followed respiratory distress protocol.
